approaches.name: Brute Force O(d) Iteration
approaches.summary: Repeatedly extract the last digit of the number using modulo 10, add it to a running sum, and remove it by integer division by 10 until the number becomes 0. This is the most straightforward method suitable for small inputs or educational purposes.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sum of Digits Using Brute Force Iteration
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of digit sum calculation using modulo and integer division operations.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires computing the sum of all digits in a given integer. The brute force O(d) iteration approach works by repeatedly extracting the last digit using modulo 10, adding it to a running total, then removing that digit via integer division by 10. The intuition is that any integer can be decomposed into its constituent digits through base-10 representation. By using modulo 10 (n % 10), we isolate the rightmost digit (units place). Adding this to a sum accumulator gives us partial results. Then integer division by 10 (n // 10) effectively shifts the number right by one digit, removing the processed digit. We repeat until n becomes 0, meaning all digits have been processed. This is O(d) time where d is the number of digits, and O(1) space since we only use a few variables. The connection to L1 is that this full solution calls the digit extraction loop repeatedly. Common mistakes include: not handling negative numbers (should take absolute value), infinite loops if not updating n properly, and integer overflow with large numbers (though Python handles big integers). Real code pattern: initialize sum = 0, while n != 0: digit = n % 10, sum += digit, n = n // 10, return sum.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Digit Extraction Loop
ladders.desc: Implement the while loop that extracts digits until number becomes zero.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine forms the core iterative process of the brute force approach. The intuition is that we need to process each digit exactly once, moving from right to left (least significant to most significant). The loop condition (n != 0) ensures we stop when no digits remain. Inside the loop: first extract last digit via n % 10, then update n via n // 10 to remove that digit. This creates a shrinking sequence: for n=123, iterations process 3, then 12 processes 2, then 1 processes 1. Time complexity is O(d) where d is digits count, space O(1). This feeds into L0 by providing the main computational engine. Common mistakes: using n > 0 (fails for negative numbers), forgetting to update n causing infinite loop, or using floating division instead of integer division. Real pattern: while n != 0: digit = n % 10; # process digit; n = n // 10.

examples.trace:
n=456
Iteration 1: digit = 456 % 10 = 6, n becomes 456 // 10 = 45
Iteration 2: digit = 45 % 10 = 5, n becomes 45 // 10 = 4
Iteration 3: digit = 4 % 10 = 4, n becomes 4 // 10 = 0
Loop ends, digits extracted in order: 6, 5, 4

n=700
Iteration 1: digit = 700 % 10 = 0, n becomes 700 // 10 = 70
Iteration 2: digit = 70 % 10 = 0, n becomes 70 // 10 = 7
Iteration 3: digit = 7 % 10 = 7, n becomes 7 // 10 = 0
Loop ends, digits extracted: 0, 0, 7

ladders.title: Core Logic — Extracting Last Digit Using Modulo
ladders.desc: Use modulo 10 operation to isolate the rightmost digit of a number.
ladders.explanation: This is the fundamental mathematical insight: in base-10 system, n % 10 gives the remainder when n is divided by 10, which corresponds exactly to the last digit. The intuition comes from place value: any integer can be expressed as d_k*10^k + ... + d_1*10 + d_0. When we take modulo 10, all terms with factor 10 become 0, leaving only d_0 (the units digit). For example, 123 = 1*100 + 2*10 + 3, so 123 % 10 = 3. This operation is O(1) time and space. It connects to L1 as the first step in each loop iteration. Common mistakes: confusing % with /, not understanding negative modulo results (in Python -123 % 10 = 7, but we typically use abs()), or applying to non-integers. Real pattern: last_digit = n % 10.

examples.trace:
47 in base-10: 4*10 + 7
47 divided by 10: quotient 4, remainder 7
47 % 10 = 7 (the remainder)

ladders.title: Building Block — Integer Division by 10 to Remove Last Digit
ladders.desc: Use floor division by 10 to discard the rightmost digit of an integer.
ladders.explanation: This operation removes the least significant digit by performing integer division (floor division) by 10. The intuition: when we divide by 10 in base-10, we effectively shift all digits one place to the right, dropping the remainder (the last digit). Mathematically, n // 10 gives the quotient when n is divided by 10, which corresponds to all digits except the last one. For example, 123 // 10 = 12, removing the 3. This is O(1) time and space. It connects to L2 as the complementary operation to modulo: modulo extracts the digit, division removes it. Common mistakes: using regular division (/) which yields float in Python, not handling negative numbers correctly (// floors toward negative infinity), or expecting it to work on strings. Real pattern: n = n // 10.

examples.trace:
456 ÷ 10 = 45.6
Floor division truncates decimal: 456 // 10 = 45
Original: 456, after removing last digit: 45

1000 ÷ 10 = 100.0
1000 // 10 = 100
Removes last digit 0, leaving 100

ladders.title: Basic Operation — While Loop with Condition
ladders.desc: Implement a while loop that continues while a condition is true.
ladders.explanation: A while loop repeatedly executes a block of code as long as a specified condition remains true. The intuition is to perform repetitive tasks without knowing exact iteration count in advance. For digit sum, we loop while n != 0 because we want to process all digits until none remain. Structure: initialize, check condition, execute body, update variables, repeat. Time depends on iterations, space O(1). This connects to L3 as the control structure that applies division repeatedly. Common mistakes: infinite loops from unchanged condition, off-by-one errors, or using wrong condition (n > 0 fails for 0). Real pattern: while condition: # do something; update variables.

examples.trace:
Initialize n=3
While n > 0: print n; n = n-1
Iteration 1: n=3 > 0, print 3, n becomes 2
Iteration 2: n=2 > 0, print 2, n becomes 1
Iteration 3: n=1 > 0, print 1, n becomes 0
Loop ends as n=0 not > 0

n=5
While n != 0: print 'Processing:', n; n = n-1
Iteration 1: n=5 != 0, print Processing:5, n=4
Iteration 2: n=4 != 0, print Processing:4, n=3
Iteration 3: n=3 != 0, print Processing:3, n=2
Iteration 4: n=2 != 0, print Processing:2, n=1
Iteration 5: n=1 != 0, print Processing:1, n=0
Loop ends

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Integer Variables and Arithmetic
ladders.desc: Understand integer data type and basic arithmetic operations.
ladders.explanation: Integers are whole numbers without fractional components, represented in programming as int data type. Basic arithmetic includes addition (+), subtraction (-), multiplication (*), division (/), and modulo (%). The intuition: computers store integers in binary, but we manipulate them using familiar decimal operations. For digit sum problem, we need to understand that integers can be operated on mathematically. Time and space are O(1) for single operations. This connects to L4 as while loops use integer conditions. Common mistakes: confusing integer vs string operations, integer overflow in some languages (not Python), or division yielding floats. Real pattern: a = 5, b = 3, sum = a + b, difference = a - b, product = a * b, quotient = a // b, remainder = a % b.

examples.trace:
a=10, b=3
Addition: 10 + 3 = 13
Subtraction: 10 - 3 = 7
Multiplication: 10 * 3 = 30
Integer division: 10 // 3 = 3 (since 3*3=9 ≤ 10)
Modulo: 10 % 3 = 1 (remainder when 10 divided by 3)

approaches.name: String Conversion O(d)
approaches.summary: Convert the number to a string, iterate over each character, convert each character back to an integer digit, and sum them. This approach is intuitive for those familiar with string handling but involves overhead from type conversions.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sum of Digits via String Conversion
ladders.desc: Complete solution to compute sum of digits by converting number to string and summing digit characters.
ladders.explanation:
This approach leverages string manipulation to extract digits from a number. The intuition is that converting an integer to its string representation naturally separates each digit into individual characters, making them accessible for processing. This method is particularly intuitive for programmers familiar with string operations, though it involves overhead from type conversions between integer and string types.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Convert the input integer to a string representation.
2. Initialize a sum variable to 0.
3. Iterate through each character in the string.
4. Convert each character back to an integer digit.
5. Add the digit to the running sum.
6. Return the final sum.

Pseudocode:
function sum_of_digits(n):
    str_n = str(n)
    total = 0
    for char in str_n:
        digit = int(char)
        total += digit
    return total

Time complexity: O(d) where d is the number of digits in n. We iterate through each digit once.
Space complexity: O(d) for storing the string representation.

Connection to higher level: This is the complete implementation of the string conversion approach.

Common mistakes:
1. Not handling negative numbers properly - the string conversion of negative numbers includes '-' character.
2. Forgetting to convert characters back to integers before summing.
3. Not initializing the sum variable.

Real code pattern:
def sum_of_digits(n):
    s = str(abs(n))  # Handle negatives
    total = 0
    for ch in s:
        total += int(ch)
    return total

examples.trace:
Step 1: Convert 123 to string: '123'
Step 2: Initialize total = 0
Step 3: Iterate through characters:
  - char '1': convert to int 1, total = 0 + 1 = 1
  - char '2': convert to int 2, total = 1 + 2 = 3
  - char '3': convert to int 3, total = 3 + 3 = 6
Step 4: Return total = 6

Step 1: Convert 9876 to string: '9876'
Step 2: Initialize total = 0
Step 3: Iterate through characters:
  - char '9': convert to int 9, total = 0 + 9 = 9
  - char '8': convert to int 8, total = 9 + 8 = 17
  - char '7': convert to int 7, total = 17 + 7 = 24
  - char '6': convert to int 6, total = 24 + 6 = 30
Step 4: Return total = 30

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — String to Digit Summation
ladders.desc: Core function that takes a string of digits and returns their sum.
ladders.explanation:
This sub-routine is the heart of the string conversion approach. The intuition is that once we have a string representation of a number, we can process it character by character, treating each character as a digit. This works because in most programming languages, characters representing digits ('0' to '9') have consecutive ASCII/Unicode values, allowing easy conversion to their integer equivalents.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize a sum variable to 0.
2. Iterate through each character in the input string.
3. For each character, convert it to its integer equivalent.
4. Add this integer to the running sum.
5. Return the final sum.

Pseudocode:
function sum_digits_from_string(s):
    total = 0
    for char in s:
        digit = char_to_int(char)
        total += digit
    return total

Time complexity: O(n) where n is the length of the string.
Space complexity: O(1) additional space beyond the input.

Connection to level above: This function is called after converting the number to string in the full solution.

Common mistakes:
1. Not validating that the string contains only digit characters.
2. Using character values directly without conversion (e.g., adding ASCII values).
3. Forgetting that string iteration gives characters, not integers.

Real code pattern:
def sum_digits_from_string(s):
    total = 0
    for ch in s:
        # ord('0') is 48, so ch - '0' gives digit value
        total += ord(ch) - ord('0')
    return total

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize total = 0
Step 2: Iterate through '123':
  - char '1': ord('1')=49, ord('0')=48, digit=49-48=1, total=0+1=1
  - char '2': ord('2')=50, digit=50-48=2, total=1+2=3
  - char '3': ord('3')=51, digit=51-48=3, total=3+3=6
Step 3: Return total = 6

Step 1: Initialize total = 0
Step 2: Iterate through '9876':
  - char '9': ord('9')=57, digit=57-48=9, total=0+9=9
  - char '8': ord('8')=56, digit=56-48=8, total=9+8=17
  - char '7': ord('7')=55, digit=55-48=7, total=17+7=24
  - char '6': ord('6')=54, digit=54-48=6, total=24+6=30
Step 3: Return total = 30

ladders.title: Core Logic — Character to Digit Conversion
ladders.desc: Convert a single character digit to its integer value.
ladders.explanation:
This is the fundamental operation that enables the string-based approach. The intuition relies on the fact that digit characters ('0' through '9') have consecutive code point values in character encoding systems like ASCII and Unicode. By subtracting the code point of '0' from any digit character, we get the integer value of that digit. This works because the encoding maintains a consistent offset between characters representing consecutive digits.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Take a character that represents a digit ('0'-'9').
2. Get its code point value (e.g., using ord() in Python).
3. Subtract the code point of '0' from this value.
4. The result is the integer value of the digit.

Pseudocode:
function char_to_digit(char):
    return code_point(char) - code_point('0')

Time complexity: O(1)
Space complexity: O(1)

Connection to level above: This conversion is applied to each character when summing digits from a string.

Common mistakes:
1. Assuming all characters are digits without validation.
2. Using the character itself instead of its code point.
3. Not understanding why subtracting '0' works.

Real code pattern:
def char_to_digit(ch):
    # In Python: ord('0') = 48, ord('1') = 49, etc.
    return ord(ch) - ord('0')

examples.trace:
Step 1: Get code point of '5': ord('5') = 53
Step 2: Get code point of '0': ord('0') = 48
Step 3: Subtract: 53 - 48 = 5
Step 4: Return 5

Step 1: Get code point of '9': ord('9') = 57
Step 2: Get code point of '0': ord('0') = 48
Step 3: Subtract: 57 - 48 = 9
Step 4: Return 9

ladders.title: Building Block — String Iteration
ladders.desc: Process each character in a string sequentially.
ladders.explanation:
String iteration is the mechanism that allows us to access individual characters in a string. The intuition is that strings are sequences of characters, and most programming languages provide ways to traverse these sequences element by element. This is essential for the string conversion approach because we need to examine each digit character separately to convert and sum them.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Start with a string input.
2. Use a loop construct to iterate through the string.
3. In each iteration, the current character is made available for processing.
4. Continue until all characters have been processed.

Pseudocode:
function iterate_string(s):
    for i from 0 to length(s)-1:
        current_char = s[i]
        # Process current_char

Time complexity: O(n) where n is string length.
Space complexity: O(1) for the iteration itself.

Connection to level above: This iteration pattern is used to apply character-to-digit conversion to each character in a string.

Common mistakes:
1. Using wrong loop bounds (off-by-one errors).
2. Modifying the string while iterating.
3. Not understanding that strings are immutable in some languages.

Real code pattern:
# Python-style iteration
for ch in s:
    # Process character ch

# Or using indices
for i in range(len(s)):
    ch = s[i]
    # Process character ch

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize iteration
Step 2: First iteration: ch = 'a'
Step 3: Second iteration: ch = 'b'
Step 4: Third iteration: ch = 'c'
Step 5: End of string reached

Step 1: Initialize iteration
Step 2: First iteration: ch = '1'
Step 3: Second iteration: ch = '2'
Step 4: Third iteration: ch = '3'
Step 5: End of string reached

testCases.expected: Characters: h, e, l, l, o
Characters: p, y, t, h, o, n

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Integer to String Conversion
ladders.desc: Convert an integer to its string representation.
ladders.explanation:
This operation transforms a numeric value into a sequence of characters that represent that number in base-10 notation. The intuition is that every integer has a canonical textual representation (e.g., 123 becomes '123'), and programming languages provide built-in functions to perform this conversion. This is the first step in the string conversion approach for summing digits, as it gives us a string where each digit is a separate character.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Take an integer input.
2. Use language's conversion function (e.g., str() in Python, toString() in Java).
3. The function internally handles the conversion by determining each digit's character.
4. Return the resulting string.

Pseudocode:
function int_to_string(n):
    return string_representation_of(n)

Time complexity: O(d) where d is number of digits, as each digit needs processing.
Space complexity: O(d) for the resulting string.

Connection to level above: This conversion provides the string that we then iterate through to extract digits.

Common mistakes:
1. Not handling negative numbers (may include '-' sign).
2. Assuming the conversion works for non-integer types.
3. Not understanding that this creates a new string object.

Real code pattern:
# Python
s = str(123)  # s becomes '123'

# Handling negatives
s = str(-456)  # s becomes '-456'
s = str(abs(-456))  # s becomes '456' if we want only digits

examples.trace:
Step 1: Input integer: 123
Step 2: Determine digits: 1, 2, 3
Step 3: Convert each digit to character: '1', '2', '3'
Step 4: Concatenate characters: '123'
Step 5: Return string '123'

Step 1: Input integer: 0
Step 2: Determine digit: 0
Step 3: Convert digit to character: '0'
Step 4: Return string '0'

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Digits and Place Value
ladders.desc: Recognize that multi-digit numbers consist of individual digits with positional values.
ladders.explanation:
This foundational concept is crucial for understanding why the string conversion approach works. The intuition is that our decimal number system is positional: each digit's value depends on its position. For example, in 123, the '1' represents 100 (1 × 10²), the '2' represents 20 (2 × 10¹), and the '3' represents 3 (3 × 10⁰). When we convert a number to a string, we're essentially extracting these digit symbols from their positional context.

Key insights:
1. Numbers are composed of digits 0-9.
2. Each digit's position determines its contribution to the total value.
3. The rightmost digit is the ones place, next is tens, then hundreds, etc.
4. String representation preserves the digit sequence but removes positional weighting.

Example analysis:
Number 456:
- Digit '4' in hundreds place: 400
- Digit '5' in tens place: 50
- Digit '6' in ones place: 6
- String '456' shows the digits in order

Connection to level above: Understanding that numbers have digit components justifies why converting to string gives us access to individual digits.

Common misunderstandings:
1. Thinking digits are the same as the full number.
2. Not recognizing place value system.
3. Confusing digit characters with their numeric values.

Real-world analogy: A street address '123 Main St' - the building number '123' consists of digits 1, 2, and 3, just as the number 123 consists of those same digits.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Identify number 123
Step 2: Break into digits: 1, 2, 3
Step 3: Determine place values:
  - Leftmost digit 1: 1 × 100 = 100 (hundreds place)
  - Middle digit 2: 2 × 10 = 20 (tens place)
  - Rightmost digit 3: 3 × 1 = 3 (ones place)
Step 4: Verify: 100 + 20 + 3 = 123

Step 1: Identify number 405
Step 2: Break into digits: 4, 0, 5
Step 3: Determine place values:
  - Leftmost digit 4: 4 × 100 = 400 (hundreds place)
  - Middle digit 0: 0 × 10 = 0 (tens place)
  - Rightmost digit 5: 5 × 1 = 5 (ones place)
Step 4: Verify: 400 + 0 + 5 = 405

approaches.name: Recursive O(d)
approaches.summary: Define a recursive function that sums the last digit (using modulo 10) with the result of calling itself on the remaining number (after integer division by 10), with a base case when the number is 0. This is elegant but may have stack overhead for large numbers.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Recursive Sum of Digits
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution to compute sum of digits of a non-negative integer.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires summing all digits of a given non-negative integer using recursion. The intuition is that we can break the number into its last digit and the remaining number, sum them recursively. For any number n, the sum of digits = (n % 10) + sum_of_digits(n // 10). The base case is when n == 0, returning 0. This approach elegantly decomposes the problem but uses O(d) stack space where d is the number of digits. Step-by-step: 1. Define function sum_digits(n). 2. If n == 0, return 0 (base case). 3. Otherwise, return (n % 10) + sum_digits(n // 10). Time: O(d) as each digit is processed once. Space: O(d) for recursion stack. Connection to L1: This directly uses the recursive helper from L1. Common mistakes: forgetting base case causing infinite recursion, not handling negative numbers (assume non-negative), integer overflow not an issue in Python. Real code pattern: def sum_digits(n): return 0 if n == 0 else (n % 10) + sum_digits(n // 10).

examples.trace: sum_digits(123) -> 123 % 10 = 3, 123 // 10 = 12 -> 3 + sum_digits(12). sum_digits(12) -> 12 % 10 = 2, 12 // 10 = 1 -> 2 + sum_digits(1). sum_digits(1) -> 1 % 10 = 1, 1 // 10 = 0 -> 1 + sum_digits(0). sum_digits(0) -> base case returns 0. Backtrack: 1 + 0 = 1, 2 + 1 = 3, 3 + 3 = 6.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Digit Extraction and Summation
ladders.desc: Recursive function that extracts last digit and sums with recursive call on remaining number.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core recursive function for summing digits. The intuition is to repeatedly peel off the last digit using modulo 10 and reduce the number by integer division by 10, summing recursively until the number becomes 0. This mimics the mathematical process of digit decomposition. Step-by-step: 1. Function rec_sum(n). 2. Base case: if n == 0, return 0. 3. Recursive case: return (n % 10) + rec_sum(n // 10). Time: O(d) where d is digits. Space: O(d) for recursion depth. Connection to L0: This is the exact function used in L0. Connection to L2: It relies on the insight of digit separation. Common mistakes: incorrect base case (e.g., n < 10), not using integer division. Real code pattern: def rec_sum(n): if n == 0: return 0; return (n % 10) + rec_sum(n // 10).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Digit Separation via Modulo and Division
ladders.desc: Technique to isolate last digit and reduce number using modulo 10 and integer division by 10.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves using modulo operator (%) to get the last digit and integer division (//) to remove it, enabling recursive processing. Intuition: Any integer n can be expressed as n = 10 * q + r, where q is quotient (n // 10) and r is remainder (n % 10), which is the last digit. By repeatedly applying this, we decompose the number digit by digit. Step-by-step: For input n, compute last_digit = n % 10, remaining = n // 10. This is the key operation in each recursive step. Time: O(1) per operation. Space: O(1). Connection to L1: This logic is applied in each recursive call. Connection to L3: Builds on arithmetic operations. Common mistakes: using float division instead of integer division, misunderstanding modulo for negative numbers (not needed here). Real code pattern: last_digit = n % 10; remaining = n // 10.

examples.trace: n = 123. n % 10 = 123 % 10 = 3 (last digit). n // 10 = 123 // 10 = 12 (remaining number).
n = 50. n % 10 = 50 % 10 = 0 (last digit). n // 10 = 50 // 10 = 5 (remaining number).

ladders.title: Building Block — Integer Division and Modulo Operations
ladders.desc: Understanding integer division (floor division) and modulo operations on integers.
ladders.explanation: This building block covers the arithmetic operations of integer division (//) and modulo (%). Intuition: Integer division gives the quotient when dividing two integers, discarding any remainder, while modulo gives the remainder. For example, 17 // 5 = 3 (since 5*3=15), and 17 % 5 = 2 (remainder). In base-10, n // 10 removes the last digit, and n % 10 extracts it. Step-by-step: For any integer n, compute quotient = n // 10, remainder = n % 10. These are fundamental for digit manipulation. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection to L2: These operations implement digit separation. Connection to L4: Relies on basic arithmetic. Common mistakes: confusing / (float division) with //, not handling negative cases (but we assume non-negative n). Real code pattern: quotient = n // 10; remainder = n % 10.

examples.trace: n = 23. Integer division: 23 divided by 10 gives quotient 2 (since 10*2=20). Modulo: remainder is 23 - 20 = 3.
n = 100. Integer division: 100 // 10 = 10 (10*10=100). Modulo: remainder is 100 - 100 = 0.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Arithmetic with Integers
ladders.desc: Performing addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division on integers.
ladders.explanation: This level covers basic integer arithmetic operations: addition (+), subtraction (-), multiplication (*), and division (/ or //). Intuition: These are fundamental mathematical operations that allow manipulation of numbers. For sum of digits, we primarily use addition to accumulate digits, and division/modulo as in L3. Step-by-step: Given two integers a and b, compute sum = a + b, difference = a - b, product = a * b, quotient = a // b (integer division). Time: O(1) for each operation. Space: O(1). Connection to L3: Division and modulo are specific cases of arithmetic. Connection to L5: Builds on number concepts. Common mistakes: integer overflow (not in Python), mixing types. Real code pattern: result = a + b; result = a - b; result = a * b; result = a // b.

examples.trace: a=7, b=3. Addition: 7 + 3 = 10. Subtraction: 7 - 3 = 4. Multiplication: 7 * 3 = 21. Integer division: 7 // 3 = 2 (since 3*2=6).
a=10, b=2. Addition: 10 + 2 = 12. Subtraction: 10 - 2 = 8. Multiplication: 10 * 2 = 20. Integer division: 10 // 2 = 5 (since 2*5=10).

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Integers and Digits
ladders.desc: Basic concept of integers as whole numbers and digits as 0-9 symbols in base-10.
ladders.explanation: This foundation introduces integers as whole numbers (e.g., 0, 1, 2, ...) and digits as the symbols 0 through 9 used in the decimal (base-10) system. Intuition: An integer like 123 represents 1 hundred + 2 tens + 3 ones, where each position is a digit. Digits are the building blocks of numbers. Step-by-step: Identify digits in a number, e.g., in 456, digits are 4, 5, 6. This is prerequisite for summing digits. Time: O(1) to understand. Space: O(1). Connection to L4: Digits are manipulated using arithmetic. Connection to higher levels: Enables decomposition of numbers. Common mistakes: confusing digits with numbers, e.g., thinking 10 is a single digit. Real code pattern: Concept only, no code; e.g., number 123 has digits 1,2,3.

examples.trace: The number 78 is composed of two digits: 7 (tens place) and 8 (ones place).
The number 205 has three digits: 2 (hundreds place), 0 (tens place), and 5 (ones place).
